text: The surgeon performs an intracorporeal esophageal jejunal anastomosis using a circular stapler, aligning the esophageal and jejunal ends for stapling.

The surgeon begins the intracorporeal esophageal jejunal stoma by clamping the distal esophagus with a laparoscopic intestinal clamp. A laparoscopic purse string clamp, known as a lap jack, is inserted through the twelve millimeter left middle trocar. The clamp is positioned as low as possible above the intestinal clamp, ensuring oncological safety.

The surgeon inserts one end of a two-headed straight needle into the upper hole of the lap jack and the other end into the lower hole, forming a purse string suture. The end of the suture is then extracted through the right upper trocar.
The surgeon transects the esophagus just below the lap jack using a hook or spatula-type bovie. After releasing the lap jack, any remaining content inside the esophagus is suctioned out.

The operator grasps the esophageal wall while the first assistant holds the opposite end. The anvil of a 25mm circular stapler is inserted into the esophagus, and the previously created purse string suture is tied using Ruder's method.
The circular stapler is opened inside the abdomen and inserted into the anvil, completing the intracorporeal esophageal jejunal stoma procedure.

The surgeon cuts the nylon tape securing the jejunum to the circular stapler. The circular stapler is fired, creating the anastomosis. The stapler is then carefully opened and withdrawn to prevent tearing of the newly formed anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a 60mm linear stapler cartridge to resect the entry hole, ensuring precise closure of the tissue.
